source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 6.6 EX commands (pp. 92-95)

questions.q: Turn ON line numbering in the current file (give the short ex command).
questions.why: :set nu (or the longer :set number) shows line numbers; :set nonu hides them again.

questions.q: Search FORWARD from the cursor for the string 'error'.
questions.why: /string searches forward from the cursor; ?string searches backward.

questions.q: Search BACKWARD (before the cursor) for the string 'error'.
questions.why: ?string searches backward from the cursor towards the start of the file.

questions.q: After a search, what do n and N do?
options: n finds the next match; N finds the previous match
n finds the previous match; N finds the next
n starts a new search; N cancels it
n numbers the lines; N unnumbers them
questions.why: n repeats the search in the same direction (next match); N repeats it in the opposite direction (previous match).

questions.q: In a VI search, what do the characters ^, $ and . mean?
options: ^ matches the start of a line, $ the end of a line, and . any single character (except newline)
^ matches the end, $ the start, and . a literal dot
^ and $ both match any character; . matches a digit
They have no special meaning in VI searches
questions.why: VI searches use regex: ^Word finds lines starting with Word, Word$ lines ending with it, W.rd matches any character in the middle, and * means the previous character repeated any number of times.

questions.q: Throughout the WHOLE file, replace every occurrence of 'foo' with 'bar' (all occurrences on every line).
questions.why: :% applies to the whole file and the trailing /g replaces every occurrence on each line, not just the first.

questions.q: In a substitution like :n,m s/search/replace, what does adding /g at the end change?
options: It replaces EVERY occurrence on each line, not just the first
It makes the search global across all open files
questions.why: By default only the first match per line is replaced; /g forces every occurrence on the line to be replaced.

questions.q: From the first line to the last line, replace 'foo' with 'bar' (first occurrence per line only).
questions.why: 1,$ is the range from the first line to the last ($); without /g only the first match on each line is replaced.

questions.q: Delete every BLANK (empty) line in the file using a global ex command.
questions.why: g is global; ^$ matches an empty line (start immediately followed by end), and d deletes the matched lines.

questions.q: Delete every line that CONTAINS the string 'debug'.
questions.why: :g/string/d deletes all lines matching the string; :g!/string/d would instead delete the lines that do NOT contain it.
